segments.s039-g25: نشستند is the past 3rd-person plural of نشستن ('to sit') and is the main verb meaning 'they sat down.' هر سه is the subject phrase 'all three' (literally 'each three') placed after the verb — a common poetic/inverted word order. به آرام و شاد is an adverbial phrase of manner formed with به + noun/adjective ('in a calm and joyful way'); به here marks the manner (–ly), and و simply connects the two qualities.
segments.s039-g26: چنان introduces a comparison or simile ('such/like so'), linking this clause to the following noun phrase. مرزبانان is the plural noun 'marzbāns' (frontier governors/guards); فرخ نژاد is a compound attributive phrase meaning 'fortunate-born' or 'of fortunate lineage' (فرخ modifying نژاد), so the whole phrase means 'marzbāns of fortunate lineage.' Together the segment reads 'like the fortunate-born marzbāns.'
segments.s039-g9: به ... اندر آورد پای is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic word order for پای را به ... اندر آورد / پای به ... اندر آورد: “to set foot on/into.” Here به + noun marks the place (‘onto the Kayan throne’), اندر آورد (past) = ‘brought/placed (his) foot’, and پای is the thing put down (‘foot’). The sequence is object/place (به تخت کیان) + verb phrase (اندر آورد پای) rather than the modern S V O order.
segments.s039-g10: همی is the classical verbal particle often used before past verbs to give continuous or iterative colouring; خواندندیش is the past plural verb خواندند plus the enclitic -یش = ‘him’ (i.e. خواندند + ایش = ‘they called/summoned him’). خاور خدای is a nominal compound/title ‘Khāvar-ḵodā’ = ‘Lord (or God) of the East’ used as the name/epithet they give him.
segments.s039-g11: دگر here functions adverbially (‘then’/‘next’/‘in addition’). تور را is Tur as definite object marked by the object marker را. داد is the past of دادن (‘gave’). توران زمین = ‘the land of Turan’ (توران as name + زمین ‘land’). The clause order is adverb + object + verb + complement.
segments.s039-g12: ورا is a pronominal/locative form here meaning ‘to him’ (i.e. the beneficiary). کرد (past of کردن) = ‘made’ (caused to be). سالار = ‘commander/lord’ and is the predicate noun; ترکان و چین = ‘the Turks and China’ are the domains over which he was made سالار. Word order: indirect object (ورا) + فعل (کرد) + predicate (سالار ...) .
segments.s039-g13: یکی لشکری نامزد کرد شاه is a compact, slightly inverted order: یکی (a certain/one) modifies لشکری (‘an army/forces’), نامزد کرد = ‘appointed/nominated’ (namazd used here as ‘appoint’), and شاه is the agent ‘the king’ (inversion places the agent at the end for emphasis). In modern Persian: شاه لشکری را نامزد کرد. The clause means ‘the king appointed a certain army.’
segments.s039-g14: آنگهی = ‘then/thereupon’. کشید لشکر (کشید + لشکر) here uses classical کشیدن in the military sense ‘to draw/bring/marshal’ or ‘to march forth’ (verb + object = ‘he marshalled/led forth an army’). تور is the agent (Tur). به راه = ‘on the road / set out.’ Word order places the verb before the subject occasionally; overall: ‘Then Tur marshalled the army and set out on the road.’
segments.s039-g15: بیامد = past tense ‘he came.’ به تخت کئی = ‘to the Kayan (i.e. Kayanian) throne’ (به marks direction/place). برنشست = past ‘sat upon’ (بر + نشستن). The clause is straightforward narrative past: ‘he came to the Kayan throne and sat upon it.’
segments.s039-g16: کمر بر میان بست is a fixed expression: کمر (waist/loins) + بر میان بست (‘he bound around the middle’) = ‘he girded his loins’ (bست = past of بستن ‘to bind’). و بگشاد دست = ‘and he opened his hands’ (بگشاد is classical past/imperfective form of گشودن ‘to open’). The sequence lists two successive actions (girding then opening his hands).
segments.s039-g1: This is a subordinate temporal clause: چو = “when”; نهفته is a participial/nominal element meaning “the hidden (thing)” and functions as the object that is being drawn out; بیرون کشید is the past tense 3rd-person singular of کشیدن (“to pull/draw out”) with the adverb/particle بیرون marking direction “out”; از نهان is a prepositional phrase “from concealment.” The subject is omitted here (understood from the main clause).
segments.s039-g2: Main clause: به سه بخش = “into/into three parts” (به + numeral + noun marks division or result), کرد is the past-tense verb “made/did” meaning “divided” here, آفریدون is the subject (Fereydun) placed after the verb in classical narrative word order, and جهان is the direct object (“the world”).
segments.s039-g3: This is the first two items of a threefold list. یکی introduces the first portion (“one [part]”); روم و خاور names the first region (“Rome and the East”); دگر (= “the other/another”) introduces the second portion, ترک و چین = “the Turks and China.” The nouns are coordinated with و (“and”).
segments.s039-g4: This continues the list with the (implicit) third portion: سیم (in the line) marks the third item, while دشت گردان is a nominal phrase meaning “the roaming/turning plains” (دشت = plain, گردان = revolving/roaming as an attributive adjective), و ایران‌زمین = “and the land/realm of Iran.” The verb is omitted because this is an appositive list continuing from the previous clause.
segments.s039-g5: نخستین = “first” (ordinal adverb). به introduces the target of his gaze (“toward/to”); سلم here is a proper name (Salm, one of Fereydun’s sons) and functions as the person he looked at (the supplied sense list does not give the proper-name sense). اندرون = “inside/within”; بنگرید is the past 3rd-person singular (بنگریـد) of نگریدن “to look/gaze,” with the subject omitted and understood from context.
segments.s039-g6: همه روم و خاور = “all Rome and the East” (the thing being evaluated). مراو را is a beneficiary/dative phrase referring back to the person for whom the domain is judged appropriate (the archaic compound مراو functions like a clitic/dative reference) and را is the object/definiteness marker; سزید is the past of سزا/سزایدن used impersonally/with a dative sense “was fitting/deserved.” The clause expresses that those lands were deemed fitting for him.
segments.s039-g7: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb meaning “he commanded/ordered.” تا introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that / in order that”), لشکری is the object “an army/forces,” and برگزید is the past of برگزیدن “to choose/select” — together: he ordered that an army be selected.
segments.s039-g8: گرازان here denotes the troop/contingent (the chosen forces); سوی خاور is a prepositional phrase “toward the east”; اندرکشید is the past 3rd-person singular of اندرکشیدن, here meaning “he drew/moved/led” (i.e., moved the troops). The verb has an implied subject (the ruler) carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s039-g17: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones / nobles'. برو here is an archaic/poetic motion verb rendered as 'went about' (an intransitive sense of 'go') that sets the scene for the following action. گوهر افشاندند is a simple past transitive clause 'scattered jewels' (گوهر = jewel, افشاندند = they scattered); word order places the verb after its object, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s039-g18: همی before the verb functions as a frequentative/intensifying particle in classical Persian poetry (here giving a narrative or emphatic flavor to the action). پاک qualifies the following title as 'pure/spotless'. توران شَهْش is a compound title 'the King of Turan' where شهش = شاه + ـش (the noun shah with a 3rd-person possessive clitic) — 'their/his king' — and خواندند is past 'they called/named' (خواندند used in the sense 'to name/call').
segments.s039-g19: از ایشان literally 'from them' = 'among them'. چو = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. نوبت 'turn' and به ایرج رسید 'reached Iraj' (رسید past tense 'arrived/was reached'), so the clause means 'when the turn came to Iraj'.
segments.s039-g20: This clause contains an archaic dative clitic (مر) meaning 'for/to him' followed by او را (the standard accusative/topic-marked 'him'), creating a doubled dative/accusative construction common in older Persian ('for him' + 'him-ACC'). پدر is the subject 'the father', گزید is the past of گزیدن 'chose', and شاه ایران is the predicate complement 'king of Iran' — literally 'the father chose him (to be) king of Iran'.
segments.s039-g21: هم ... و هم is the correlative 'both ... and ...' construction. ایران is the first item, دشت نیزه‌وران 'the plain of the spearmen' is the second; in poetry the genitive relation is expressed by juxtaposition (دشت + نیزه‌وران) without an explicit ezāfe.
segments.s039-g22: Again هم begins the second paired element 'and also'. آن 'that' points to a specific throne; تخت شاهی literally 'throne of kingship' (شاهی here functioning as the royal/sovereign quality), و links to تاج سران 'and the crown of the chiefs/leaders' (سران = leaders). The nouns are listed in a compact poetic sequence without connecting particles.
segments.s039-g23: بدو is a dative 'to him/unto him', داد is the past 'gave', and کورا is a place/name (Kura) given as the direct object. The clause then continues with سزا بود تاج — 'the crown was deserved' — where سزا 'desert/that which is due' and بود (past of 'to be') form a copular phrase 'was due', and تاج 'the crown' is the predicate; the sequence shows poetic inversion (predicate elements can precede or follow بود).
segments.s039-g24: A simple list of royal insignia: همان 'that same/the very' modifies کرسی (seat/throne), و links مهر (here 'seal' or imperial stamp) and آن تخت عاج 'that ivory throne' (آن = that, عاج = ivory). The nouns are coordinated by و in a straightforward enumerative sequence.

sentences.s039-s13: All three of them sat down calm and joyful, like fortunate-born frontier governors.
sentences.s039-s5: He set foot on the Kayan throne; they called him “Lord of the East.”
sentences.s039-s6: Then he gave Tur the land of Turan and made him lord (commander) of the Turks and China.
sentences.s039-s7: The king appointed an army; then Tur marshalled his host and set out on the road.
sentences.s039-s8: He came to the Kayan throne and sat upon it; he girded his loins and opened his hands.
sentences.s039-s1: When Fereydun drew the hidden thing out of concealment, he divided the world into three parts.
sentences.s039-s2: One (part) for Rome and the East, another for the Turks and China, the third the roaming plains and the land of Iran.
sentences.s039-s3: First he looked toward Salm; he judged that all Rome and the East were fitting for him.
sentences.s039-s4: He ordered that an army be chosen; he drew (or led) the forces toward the east.
sentences.s039-s9: The great men went about and scattered jewels; they called him the pure King of Turan.
sentences.s039-s10: When the turn came to Iraj among them, his father chose him king of Iran.
sentences.s039-s11: Both Iran and the plain of the spearmen, and likewise that royal throne and the crown of the chiefs.
sentences.s039-s12: They gave him Kura; the crown was his due — that same seat, the imperial seal, and that ivory throne.
